title: The Miles, the Metrics, and the Money
context: A cross-functional steering discussion has been called for the next wave of Expenses capabilities. The Payroll Director owns reimbursement rates. The Data Lead wants event capture that supports future analytics, the Fraud Lead wants anomaly detection on the roadmap, and the Integrations Manager is under pressure from partners who expect cleaner downstream flows. The architect must keep the room from solving four different problems with one heavy answer.

questions.prompt: The Payroll Director says: "Mileage rates already change in Payroll. Expenses should consume them rather than own a second rate table." How do you respond?

options.text: We should mirror the rates into Expenses and let the team manage them there for speed. A local copy gives the module more autonomy, removes a dependency on Payroll, and makes it easier to change the experience later. It also gives leadership a bigger integration-and-insight story and postpones the ownership trade-offs until downstream commitments already exist.
options.explanation: The autonomy concern is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it creates duplicate ownership before testing whether the business really wants a second source for the same commercial rule.

options.text: I would keep rate ownership with Payroll and design Expenses to consume that authority in a way that meets the user experience need. If latency or resilience becomes a concern, we can solve that with caching or a read model without moving rule ownership into a second domain.
options.explanation: This demonstrates applying ownership and latency criteria together. The answer keeps the business authority clear while still acknowledging the operational need for a good user experience.

options.text: That dependency sounds inconvenient, so I would avoid building mileage in phase one. If Payroll remains the owner, it may be simpler to keep mileage outside Expenses until a later programme can redesign the boundary properly.
options.explanation: The concern about dependency is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it treats a manageable boundary decision as a reason to defer the capability instead of framing a workable integration approach.

questions.prompt: The Data Lead says: "Anomaly detection is later, so background event capture can wait too. We should not design data collection until the analytics feature is funded." What do you say?

options.text: That is fair. If anomaly detection is not in the immediate release, there is no reason to think about the event shape now, and we can revisit the data model when the analytics work becomes real.
options.explanation: The desire to avoid speculative work is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: it ignores the architect's chance to separate event capture from downstream analytics platform decisions.

options.text: I would separate operational event capture from the eventual analytics implementation. If fraud and insight use cases are credible next steps, we should emit the right events now while keeping the analytics platform choice open. Capturing events is a lighter decision than committing to a full analytics stack.
options.explanation: This demonstrates separating operational reporting needs from later cross-domain analytics choices. The answer protects future reuse without letting the data platform debate take over the meeting.

options.text: If analytics matters at all, we should probably design the full pipeline now, including the lakehouse model, anomaly engine inputs, and downstream reporting semantics. That avoids redesign once the fraud use case arrives. It also gives leadership a bigger integration-and-insight story and postpones the ownership trade-offs until downstream commitments already exist.
options.explanation: The wish to avoid rework is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it uses a plausible future use case to justify a much heavier programme than the current question requires.

questions.prompt: The Integrations Manager says: "Customers will expect approved mileage claims to flow straight into every finance and payroll package we support. Let us plan direct connectors to all major vendors now." How do you respond?

options.text: That is the right ambition, and we should commit to the connector roadmap now. The fastest way to satisfy the market is to begin building direct integrations for the full vendor set rather than inserting another layer in the middle. It also gives leadership a bigger integration-and-insight story and postpones the ownership trade-offs until downstream commitments already exist.
options.explanation: The market pressure is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts a broad connector commitment before separating internal handoff architecture from external partner delivery choices.

options.text: I would separate the internal contract from the external connector strategy. The core architecture question is how approved claims leave Expenses cleanly. Once that boundary is stable, we can decide which vendor connectors are worth productising and which belong to partners or implementation layers.
options.explanation: This demonstrates naming the domain and keeping a platform decision from swallowing a product one. The answer prevents external connector pressure from dictating the internal architecture prematurely.

options.text: We need a wider partner strategy review before we can say anything meaningful here. Until Commercial and Partnerships agree the target list, there is no architectural answer to give.
options.explanation: The wish for alignment is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can still frame the internal boundary now without pretending the entire partner roadmap must be settled first.

questions.prompt: The Fraud Lead says: "If anomaly detection is on the roadmap, make it real-time so suspicious mileage claims are scored before employees submit them." What is your response?

options.text: Yes, if we care about fraud we should do the scoring before submission. That is the strongest control point, and real-time detection will also give the programme a more strategic story for leadership.
options.explanation: The control instinct is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts 'real-time' as the answer before translating whether the actual requirement is pre-submit blocking, fast review, or better post-submit visibility.

options.text: I would translate 'real-time' into the actual decision point we need. If the near-term need is rapid review or prioritisation, near-real-time post-submission scoring may be enough. We should only move the score into the submission path if the risk model truly needs that latency and the false-positive cost is acceptable.
options.explanation: This demonstrates translating a slogan into a requirement and anchoring on latency. The answer keeps the room from assuming that faster always means better architecture.

options.text: Fraud models are uncertain anyway, so I would keep anomaly detection out of the design conversation entirely until we have a mature data science team ready to own it.
options.explanation: The caution about model quality is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it avoids the hard question instead of framing the latency and control trade-off in a useful way.

questions.prompt: The group asks you to close. Which summary is strongest?

options.text: We are keeping payroll-owned rates authoritative, capturing events now without forcing an analytics platform decision, separating the internal approved-claim contract from any broader connector roadmap, and treating anomaly detection latency as a requirement question rather than a slogan. That gives each team a clearer boundary for the next design step.
options.explanation: This demonstrates strong meeting closure. The answer summarises the decisions and the framing logic behind them, so the room leaves with aligned boundaries rather than a bundle of partial ideas.

options.text: There are too many adjacent topics here, so the better call is to avoid deciding anything until the fraud, data, and integrations roadmaps are all fully reconciled. A single consolidated plan will be cleaner.
options.explanation: The desire for coherence is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect's job is to separate adjacent concerns so the team can move now, not wait for one perfect mega-plan.

options.text: To stay ahead of future demand, we should design mileage as a broad platform initiative that includes local rate ownership, streaming analytics, universal vendor connectors, and pre-submit fraud scoring from the outset. That way we will not have to revisit the architecture later.
options.explanation: The future-proofing instinct is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it accepts every adjacent concern at once and turns a focused capability discussion into a much larger platform programme.
